did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a novel 'Unit-vise deep shallow residual neural network with transition layer' (a deep learning architecture) to perform expert-level skin cancer classification. The method was evaluated on the ISIC-2018 challenge dataset, achieving 98.05% accuracy.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work proposing a novel 'Unit-vise deep shallow residual neural network' architecture. It details the methodology, including network design, data augmentation (up-sampling/down-sampling), and evaluation on the ISIC-2018 challenge dataset (HAM10000), reporting new results such as 98.05% accuracy and comparative metrics against state-of-the-art models.

title: Unit-vise: Deep Shallow Unit-Vise Residual Neural Networks with Transition Layer For Expert Level Skin Cancer Classification
journal: IEEE/ACM Transactions on Computational Biology and Bioinformatics
brief_summary: This study addresses the task of expert-level skin cancer classification using a novel Unit-vise deep shallow residual neural network with transition layers. The proposed architecture divides the network into stages with dense connected residual units to enforce shorter connections and improve feature representation. The model was evaluated on the ISIC-2018 challenge dataset, specifically the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 training images across seven diagnostic categories. To handle class imbalance, the authors employed up-sampling for minority classes and various data augmentation techniques such as resizing, flipping, and rotation. The evaluation results demonstrated considerable improvement over existing approaches, achieving an accuracy of 98.05% on the training set. This performance outperformed previous state-of-the-art results achieved in the ISIC-17 and ISIC-18 skin cancer competitions.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the seven diagnostic categories in the HAM10000 dataset used for classification. Original count: 327 images; upsampled count: 6705 images.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of skin lesions (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Melanocytic Nevus, Vascular Lesion)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs expert-level skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories (multiclass classification) for skin lesions. Evaluation metrics include accuracy (98.05%), AUC, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and F-score. The task is explicitly defined as classifying images into one of seven classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset released by the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) for the 2018 challenge. It contains images of skin lesions used for expert-level skin cancer classification.

use.training: Used for training the Unit-vise deep shallow residual neural network. The dataset was up-sampled to balance class distribution (totaling 46,935 images after up-sampling) and augmented via resizing, flipping, rotation, and shear.
use.validation: Null. The paper states that 5-fold cross-validation was performed on the training set to generalize results, but does not report a separate held-out validation set count or metrics distinct from the training evaluation.
use.testing: Null. The paper reports results on the training set (achieving 98.05% accuracy) and compares against ISIC-2018 challenge leaderboards, but does not report specific metrics on a separate test set within the paper's own experimental results.
use.other: Used for comparison with state-of-the-art methods and previous ISIC challenges (ISIC-17, ISIC-18).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To generalize the results of the proposed Unit-vise network on the HAM10000 dataset.
To handle class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset by replicating samples from minority classes to equalize the number of samples with the majority class.
Mentioned as an approach to balance sample numbers, though up-sampling was primarily used in the final experimental setup.
To improve sample size and model robustness, including random cropping, flipping, rotation, shear, and color adjustments.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using descriptive metrics (accuracy, AUC, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F-score) and direct numerical comparison against state-of-the-art methods and ISIC challenge leaderboards.

HAM10000 (ISIC-2018 Challenge Dataset).notes: The proposed 'Unit-vise with Transition layer and Supervision Block' achieved 98.05% accuracy. This result was obtained using 5-fold cross-validation on the training set (10,015 images) after up-sampling minority classes to balance the dataset. The paper notes that this performance outperformed the winner of the ISIC-2018 competition (who achieved 98.3% AUC/0.983 primary metric value) and previous state-of-the-art results from ISIC-17 and ISIC-18 challenges.

ISIC-2018 Competition Leaderboard Compar.notes: The proposed Unit-vise model achieved a primary metric value (AUC) of 0.901, surpassing the ISIC-2018 winner (0.885) and runner-up (0.882). The paper highlights that the proposed method achieved 98.05% accuracy on the training set, which is higher than the accuracy reported for the competition winner (98.3% AUC, but accuracy not explicitly listed as >98.05 in the same format, though AUC is higher). Note: Table 5 lists Unit-vise Accuracy as 0.984 (98.4%) in this specific comparison table, while Table 3 and Abstract report 98.05%. The text states '98.05% accuracy' multiple times.

Comparison with Other State-of-the-Art M.notes: The proposed Unit-vise model outperformed these listed state-of-the-art methods in terms of accuracy.

architecture_used.main_model: Unit-vise: Deep Shallow Unit-Vise Residual Neural Networks with Transition Layer
architecture_used.pretraining: Trained from scratch (weights initialized as in [22], likely He initialization given the context of residual networks, though specific initialization name not explicitly stated beyond 'as in [22]'). No ImageNet pretraining reported for the proposed Unit-vise model.
architecture_used.input_size: Not explicitly reported in text (standard dermoscopic images from HAM10000 dataset, typically 224x224 or similar, but not specified).

architecture_details.family: Residual Neural Network (ResNet) variant with Dense Connectivity
architecture_details.structure: Multistage network with dense connected residual units, transition layers, and supervision blocks.
architecture_details.transition_layer: 1x1 convolutional layer and 2x2 average pooling layer
architecture_details.supervision_block: 1x1 and 3x3 convolutional layers followed by batch normalization and ReLU layer
architecture_details.skip_connections: Dense connectivity within units; projection shortcuts used when dimension changes between stages (identity mapping with zero padding)
architecture_details.final_layers: Average pooling layer (filter width 1) before fully connected layer and softmax layer

training_details.optimizer: Stochastic Gradient Descent (implied by momentum usage)
training_details.loss_function: Cross-entropy (implied by softmax layer and classification task), with supervision blocks adding auxiliary loss terms
training_details.early_stopping: Stopped when no improvement in validation error rate for 30 epochs
data_augmentation: Up-sampling minority classes to balance dataset (total 46,935 images)
Down-sampling (mentioned as approach, but up-sampling primarily used)

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported, but described as 'few numbers of channels', 'compact and thinner', and 'much fewer parameters in comparison to its counter networks' (ResNet).

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Unit-vise: Deep Shallow Unit-Vise Residual Neural Networks with Transition Layer
Unit-vise with Transition layer and Supervision Block
Ensemble of resnet50, densenet121 and mobilenet
preprocessing_techniques: Class up-sampling (replicating samples from minority classes to balance dataset)
Class down-sampling (mentioned as an approach to balance sample numbers)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Replicating samples from minority classes to equalize the number of samples with the majority class (Melanocytic Nevus, 6705 images). Applied to classes: Actinic Keratosis (327 to 6705), Basal Cell Carcinoma (514 to 6705), Benign Keratosis (1099 to 6705), Dermatofibroma (115 to 6705), Melanoma (1113 to 6705), and Vascular Lesion (142 to 6705). Total images increased from 10,015 to 46,935.

Mentioned as an approach to balance sample numbers by reducing majority class samples to match minority classes via random selection, though noted as likely to produce poor results due to information loss. Not primarily used in the final experimental setup compared to up-sampling.
Applied as a data augmentation technique to improve sample size.
Random vertical and horizontal flipping applied.
Adjustments to contrast, saturation, and hue.

conclusions: The proposed Unit-vise deep shallow residual neural network with transition and supervision blocks achieved 98.05% accuracy on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming state-of-the-art methods and previous ISIC challenge winners.

The Unit-vise architecture, which divides the network into stages with dense connected residual units and limited skip connectivity, results in a more compact and thinner model with fewer parameters compared to traditional ResNets.
The use of dense connectivity within units and transition layers enforces shorter connections, leading to better feature representation and smoother decision boundaries.

The proposed method effectively handles class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset through up-sampling of minority classes and data augmentation techniques, achieving superior performance without relying on prior knowledge of the data or post-processing.

The Unit-vise network demonstrates that multistage unit-vise deep residual networks can achieve expert-level skin cancer classification performance, surpassing the accuracy of dermatologists (65%-80%) and other deep learning models like ResNet50 (92.08%) and PNASNet-5-Large (76%).
The study acknowledges that performance could potentially be further improved by incorporating additional clinical information such as patient age and gender.
important_remarks: The primary performance metric (98.05% accuracy) was reported on the training set using 5-fold cross-validation, not on a held-out test set, which limits the assessment of generalization to unseen data.

The study addresses class imbalance by up-sampling minority classes to match the majority class (Melanocytic Nevus), resulting in a total of 46,935 images; this synthetic balancing may inflate performance metrics compared to real-world distribution.

The dataset (HAM10000) is highly imbalanced, with Dermatofibroma having only 115 original samples compared to 6,705 for Melanocytic Nevus, potentially biasing the model towards the majority class if not for the up-sampling intervention.
Ground truth for lesions was not uniformly verified by pathologists; for many samples, it relied on follow-up, expert consensus, or in-vivo confocal microscopy, introducing potential label noise.
The paper acknowledges that performance could be improved by incorporating additional clinical information (e.g., patient age, gender), which was not included in the current model.

Dermatologist accuracy is cited as 65%-80% (unaided) to 84% (with technical support), providing a baseline for comparison, but the direct comparison is between the AI model and historical challenge leaderboards rather than a concurrent clinical study.
The model was trained from scratch without ImageNet pretraining, which is a notable strength but also a constraint regarding computational resources and data efficiency.
The code for the Unit-vise network is publicly available, supporting reproducibility.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset (part of the ISIC-2018 challenge) for all experimental phases. The dataset, containing 10,015 dermoscopic images across seven diagnostic categories, was used for training, validation (via cross-validation folds), and internal testing. The authors performed class up-sampling to balance the dataset, increasing the total image count to 46,935. Performance was evaluated using 5-fold cross-validation on this training set. The results were compared against the ISIC-2018 competition leaderboard and other state-of-the-art methods, but no external or unseen test dataset was used for final evaluation.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper did not perform cross-dataset validation. The model was trained and tested on the same dataset (HAM10000) using 5-fold cross-validation. Comparisons with other works were based on leaderboard scores or reported metrics from other studies, not on testing the proposed model on those external datasets.

training.notes: Used for training the Unit-vise deep shallow residual neural network. The dataset was up-sampled to balance class distribution (replicating minority class samples to match the majority class, Melanocytic Nevus). Data augmentation techniques including resizing, flipping, rotation, shear, and color adjustments were also applied.

validation.notes: Used for model selection and tuning via 5-fold cross-validation. The paper states that training was stopped when there was no improvement in the error rate of the validation set for 30 epochs.

testing.notes: Used for final performance evaluation via 5-fold cross-validation on the training set. The reported accuracy of 98.05% is derived from this internal cross-validation process.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The paper states the network was trained from scratch. It does not mention using ImageNet or other pre-trained weights.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: Class up-sampling was used to replicate samples from minority classes (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Melanoma, Vascular Lesion) to equalize the number of samples with the majority class (Melanocytic Nevus, 6,705 images). Data augmentation included random cropping, flipping, rotation (up to 90 degrees), shear (up to 20 degrees), and color adjustments.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: 5-fold cross-validation performed on the HAM10000 training set (10,015 images). No separate held-out test set was used for the final reported results.
evaluation_protocol.cross_validation_details: 5-fold cross-validation. Folds stayed within the single HAM10000 dataset.
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used for testing. The study compares its results to the ISIC-2018 competition leaderboard, but does not test its own model on the ISIC-2018 test set separately from the training data in this paper.

dataset_level_results.model_or_method: Unit-vise with Transition layer and Supervision Block
dataset_level_results.notes: Results obtained via 5-fold cross-validation on the up-sampled HAM10000 dataset. The accuracy is reported as 98.05% in the abstract and text, while Table 5 lists it as 98.4% (0.984) in the comparison table. The AUC of 0.901 is highlighted as the primary metric.

models: Unit-vise with Transition layer and Supervision Block

results.model: Unit-vise with Transition layer and Supervision Block

generalizability_notes: The study relies solely on the HAM10000 dataset, which is highly imbalanced (though corrected via up-sampling).
Performance metrics (98.05% accuracy) are reported on the training set using cross-validation, not on a held-out test set, which limits the assessment of generalization to truly unseen data.
The ground truth for many lesions was not uniformly verified by pathologists (relying on follow-up, expert consensus, or in-vivo confocal microscopy), introducing potential label noise.
The model was trained from scratch without ImageNet pretraining.
No external validation on a separate cohort was performed.
information_not_reported: Specific counts for validation and test sets within the 5-fold CV splits.
Patient-level separation details (whether images from the same patient appear in different folds).
Results on a separate, held-out test set distinct from the training data.
